annotations.label: Divergent series

anchor.snippet: a divergent series is an infinite series that is not convergent

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Summable`/`HasSum` for convergent series; divergent series are expressed as `¬ Summable` rather than given a dedicated predicate.

annotations.label: Terms of convergent series approach zero

anchor.snippet: If a series converges, the individual terms of the series must approach zero.

annotations.note: `Summable.tendsto_atTop_zero` states that a summable sequence on ℕ tends to zero.

annotations.label: Harmonic series diverges

anchor.snippet: A counterexample is the harmonic series

annotations.note: `Real.not_summable_one_div_natCast` and the companion `Real.tendsto_sum_range_one_div_nat_succ_atTop` explicitly state divergence of the harmonic series.

annotations.label: Summability method

anchor.snippet: A summability method or summation method is a partial function from the set of series to values.

annotations.note: Mathlib's `SummationFilter` parameterizes how partial sums are taken (via a filter on finite subsets), giving infrastructure for alternative summation notions, but no general API for divergent-series summability methods is formalized.

annotations.label: Cesàro sum of Grandi's series

anchor.snippet: Cesàro summation assigns Grandi's divergent series

annotations.note: No declaration computes the Cesàro sum of Grandi's series 1 − 1 + 1 − 1 + ⋯.

annotations.label: Grandi's series 1 − 1 + 1 − 1 + ⋯
annotations.note: Grep finds no Mathlib statement about non-summability of (-1)^n as a divergent series example.
No Mathlib declaration addresses this specific divergent alternating series.
No Mathlib declaration handles this alternating factorial series.
No specific Mathlib decl for this divergent geometric series example.
No dedicated Mathlib statement for this divergent geometric series example.
`Finite.of_summable_const` implies that the constant sequence 1 over ℕ is not summable, giving the divergence of 1+1+1+⋯ as a corollary.
No Mathlib decl explicitly states divergence of 1+2+3+⋯ (its zeta-regularized value is separately covered).
No dedicated statement about the sum of squares as a divergent series.

annotations.label: Regular summability method

anchor.section: Theorems on methods for summing divergent series
anchor.snippet: A summability method M is regular if it agrees with the actual limit on all convergent series

annotations.note: No general definition of a regular summability method appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abelian theorem

anchor.section: Theorems on methods for summing divergent series
anchor.snippet: Such a result is called an Abelian theorem for M

annotations.note: Specific Abelian theorems (e.g. Abel's limit theorem) are formalized, but the abstract notion is not.

annotations.label: Tauberian theorem

anchor.section: Theorems on methods for summing divergent series
anchor.snippet: More subtle, are partial converse results, called Tauberian theorems

annotations.note: Grep finds no Tauberian framework or theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Banach limit (Hahn–Banach extension)

anchor.section: Theorems on methods for summing divergent series
anchor.snippet: it follows from the Hahn–Banach theorem that it may be extended to a summation method summing any series with bounded partial sums

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Hahn–Banach theorem but no Banach-limit construction extending the limit functional to bounded sequences.

annotations.label: Regularity

anchor.snippet: Regularity . A summation method is regular if, whenever the sequence s converges to x

annotations.note: No abstract regularity property of summation methods is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linearity

anchor.snippet: Linearity . A is linear if it is a linear functional on the sequences where it is defined

annotations.note: No abstract linearity property of summation methods is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stability (translativity)

anchor.snippet: Stability (also called translativity )

annotations.note: No abstract stability/translativity property of summation methods is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite re-indexability

anchor.snippet: Finite re-indexability .

annotations.note: Not formalized — no general summation-method framework where this property can be stated.

annotations.label: Consistency of summation methods

anchor.snippet: A and B are consistent if for every sequence s to which both assign a value

annotations.note: Consistency between two summation methods is not formalized.

annotations.label: Stronger summation method

anchor.snippet: the one summing more series is stronger

annotations.note: No comparison of summation methods by strength is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geometric series sum under regular, linear, stable methods

anchor.snippet: any summation method that possesses these properties and which assigns a finite value to the geometric series must assign this value

annotations.note: Not formalized; the corresponding abstract summation-method framework is absent.

annotations.label: Cauchy's sum of a series

anchor.snippet: Cauchy's classical definition of the sum of a series

annotations.note: `HasSum`/`tsum` realize Cauchy's classical sum-as-limit-of-partial-sums definition.

annotations.label: Absolute convergence

anchor.snippet: Absolute convergence defines the sum of a sequence

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses absolute convergence as `Summable (fun x => ‖f x‖)` rather than a separate predicate, with `Summable.norm`/`Summable.abs` as the canonical witnesses.

annotations.label: Absolute convergence iff sequence of absolute values converges

anchor.snippet: a classical theorem says that a sequence is absolutely convergent if and only if the sequence of absolute values is convergent in the standard sense

annotations.note: `summable_norm_iff` (and the order-theoretic `summable_abs_iff`) give exactly this equivalence.

annotations.label: Nørlund mean

anchor.snippet: the limit of t n as n goes to infinity is an average called the Nørlund mean

annotations.note: No Nørlund-mean definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nørlund means are regular, linear, stable, and pairwise consistent

anchor.snippet: The Nørlund mean is regular, linear, and stable.

annotations.note: Not formalized — depends on absent Nørlund-mean definition.

annotations.label: Cesàro sum C_k

anchor.snippet: then the Cesàro sum C k is defined by

annotations.note: Mathlib has Cesàro averaging of a convergent sequence (`Filter.Tendsto.cesaro`/`cesaro_smul`) but no general Cesàro sum `C_k` definition for divergent series.

annotations.label: C_h stronger than C_k for h > k

anchor.snippet: Cesàro sums have the property that if h > k , then C h is stronger than C k

annotations.note: Not formalized; higher-order Cesàro sums are not defined.

annotations.label: Abelian mean

anchor.snippet: Then the Abelian mean A λ is defined as

annotations.note: No Abelian-mean construction found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Abelian means are regular and linear

anchor.snippet: Abelian means are regular and linear, but not stable and not always consistent between different choices

annotations.note: Not formalized — depends on absent Abelian-mean definition.

annotations.label: Abel summation

anchor.snippet: If λ n = n , then we obtain the method of Abel summation

annotations.note: Abel's limit theorem (showing Abel summation is regular on convergent series) is formalized, but Abel summation is not introduced as a stand-alone divergent-series method.

annotations.label: Abel sum consistent with and stronger than Cesàro

anchor.snippet: Abel summation is interesting in part because it is consistent with but more powerful than Cesàro summation

annotations.note: Comparison between Abel and Cesàro summation as divergent-series methods is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lindelöf sum

anchor.snippet: Then L ( s ), the Lindelöf sum

annotations.note: No Lindelöf summation method in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lindelöf sum in the Mittag-Leffler star

anchor.snippet: If g ( z ) is analytic in a disk around zero, and hence has a Maclaurin series

annotations.note: Not formalized — Mittag-Leffler star and Lindelöf sum are absent.

annotations.label: Sum by analytic continuation of a power series

anchor.snippet: If Σ a n x n converges for small complex x and can be analytically continued along some path

annotations.note: Mathlib has analytic continuation but not the construction assigning sums to divergent power series via continuation.

annotations.label: Callet's example of path-dependent sums

anchor.snippet: One of the first examples of potentially different sums for a divergent series, using analytic continuation, was given by Callet

annotations.note: Callet's example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Divergent alternating hypergeometric series

anchor.snippet: Another example of analytic continuation is the divergent alternating series

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler (E, q) summation

anchor.snippet: Euler summation is essentially an explicit form of analytic continuation

annotations.note: Euler (E, q) summation is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum via analytic continuation of Dirichlet series

anchor.snippet: This method defines the sum of a series to be the value of the analytic continuation of the Dirichlet series

annotations.note: L-series infrastructure exists, but it is not packaged as a generic summability method via Dirichlet-series continuation.

annotations.label: Zeta regularized sum

anchor.snippet: its value at s = −1 is called the zeta regularized sum of the series

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `riemannZeta` but does not introduce the zeta-regularization summability method for arbitrary series.

annotations.label: Zeta regularization of 1 + 2 + 3 + ⋯ = −1/12

anchor.snippet: assigning a value to the divergent series 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + ⋯

annotations.note: `riemannZeta_neg_nat_eq_bernoulli` evaluates ζ(−k) in terms of Bernoulli numbers; at k=1 it gives the −1/12 value, though it is not packaged as a divergent-series regularization.

annotations.label: J sum (integral function mean)

anchor.snippet: then the J sum of the series a 0 + ... is defined to be

annotations.note: J-sum / integral-function-mean construction is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Borel summation (weak form)

anchor.snippet: In the special case when J ( x ) = e x this gives one (weak) form of Borel summation

annotations.note: No Borel summation definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Valiron's method

anchor.snippet: Valiron's method is a generalization of Borel summation to certain more general integral functions

annotations.note: Valiron's summation method is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: (dμ) sum (moment method)

anchor.snippet: the ( dμ ) sum of the series is defined to be the value of the integral

annotations.note: Moment-method summation is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Borel summation via dμ = e^{−x} dx

anchor.snippet: if dμ = e − x dx for positive x and 0 for negative x then μ n = n !

annotations.note: Borel summation via the moment method is not formalized.

annotations.label: (B′, α) sum

anchor.snippet: called the (B′, α ) sum

annotations.note: (B′, α) summation method is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BGN hyperreal summation

anchor.snippet: This summation method works by using an extension to the real numbers known as the hyperreal numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Hyperreal` but no BGN summation method built atop it.

annotations.label: Hutton's method

anchor.snippet: In 1812 Hutton introduced a method of summing divergent series by starting with the sequence of partial sums

annotations.note: Hutton's averaging method for divergent series is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ingham summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 1 + ... is called Ingham summable to s

annotations.note: Ingham summability is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ingham's theorem relating Cesàro and Ingham summability

anchor.snippet: Albert Ingham showed that if δ is any positive number

annotations.note: Not formalized — depends on absent Ingham-summability definition.

annotations.label: Lambert summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 1 + ... is called Lambert summable to s

annotations.note: Lambert summability is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cesàro ⇒ Lambert ⇒ Abel summability

anchor.snippet: If a series is (C, k ) (Cesàro) summable for any k then it is Lambert summable to the same value

annotations.note: Not formalized — depends on absent Lambert summability.

annotations.label: Le Roy summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 0 + ... is called Le Roy summable to s

annotations.note: Le Roy summation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mittag-Leffler (M) summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 0 + ... is called Mittag-Leffler (M) summable to s

annotations.note: Mittag-Leffler summability is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ramanujan summation

anchor.snippet: Ramanujan summation is a method of assigning a value to divergent series used by Ramanujan

annotations.note: Ramanujan summation is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: (R, k) Riemann summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 1 + ... is called (R, k ) (or Riemann) summable to s

annotations.note: Riemann (R, k) summability is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: R_2 summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 1 + ... is called R 2 summable to s

annotations.note: R_2 summability is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riesz (R, λ, κ) sum

anchor.snippet: then the Riesz (R, λ , κ ) sum of the series a 0 + ... is defined to be

annotations.note: Riesz means as a summation method are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vallée-Poussin (VP) summable

anchor.snippet: The series a 1 + ... is called VP (or Vallée-Poussin) summable to s

annotations.note: Vallée-Poussin summability is not formalized.

annotations.label: Zeldovich summable

anchor.snippet: The series is Zeldovich summable

annotations.note: Zeldovich summability is not formalized in Mathlib.
